      14              :  * INTERFACE ROUTINES
      15              :  *              heap_beginscan  - begin relation scan
      16              :  *              heap_rescan             - restart a relation scan
      17              :  *              heap_endscan    - end relation scan
      18              :  *              heap_getnext    - retrieve next tuple in scan
      19              :  *              heap_fetch              - retrieve tuple with given tid
      20              :  *              heap_insert             - insert tuple into a relation
      21              :  *              heap_multi_insert - insert multiple tuples into a relation
      22              :  *              heap_delete             - delete a tuple from a relation
      23              :  *              heap_update             - replace a tuple in a relation with another tuple
      24              :  *
      25              :  * NOTES
      26              :  *        This file contains the heap_ routines which implement
      27              :  *        the POSTGRES heap access method used for all POSTGRES
      28              :  *        relations.
      29              :  *
      30              :  *-------------------------------------------------------------------------
      31              :  */

     120              : /*
     121              :  * Each tuple lock mode has a corresponding heavyweight lock, and one or two
     122              :  * corresponding MultiXactStatuses (one to merely lock tuples, another one to
     123              :  * update them).  This table (and the macros below) helps us determine the
     124              :  * heavyweight lock mode and MultiXactStatus values to use for any particular
     125              :  * tuple lock strength.
     126              :  *
     127              :  * These interact with InplaceUpdateTupleLock, an alias for ExclusiveLock.
     128              :  *
     129              :  * Don't look at lockstatus/updstatus directly!  Use get_mxact_status_for_lock
     130              :  * instead.
     131              :  */
     132              : static const struct
     133              : {
     134              :         LOCKMODE        hwlock;
     135              :         int                     lockstatus;
     136              :         int                     updstatus;
     137              : }
     138              : 
     139              :                         tupleLockExtraInfo[MaxLockTupleMode + 1] =
     140              : {
     141              :         {                                                       /* LockTupleKeyShare */
     142              :                 AccessShareLock,
     143              :                 MultiXactStatusForKeyShare,
     144              :                 -1                                              /* KeyShare does not allow updating tuples */
     145              :         },
     146              :         {                                                       /* LockTupleShare */
     147              :                 RowShareLock,
     148              :                 MultiXactStatusForShare,
     149              :                 -1                                              /* Share does not allow updating tuples */
     150              :         },
     151              :         {                                                       /* LockTupleNoKeyExclusive */
     152              :                 ExclusiveLock,
     153              :                 MultiXactStatusForNoKeyUpdate,
     154              :                 MultiXactStatusNoKeyUpdate
     155              :         },
     156              :         {                                                       /* LockTupleExclusive */
     157              :                 AccessExclusiveLock,
     158              :                 MultiXactStatusForUpdate,
     159              :                 MultiXactStatusUpdate
     160              :         }
     161              : };
     162              : 
     163              : /* Get the LOCKMODE for a given MultiXactStatus */
     164              : #define LOCKMODE_from_mxstatus(status) \
     165              :                         (tupleLockExtraInfo[TUPLOCK_from_mxstatus((status))].hwlock)
     166              : 
     167              : /*
     168              :  * Acquire heavyweight locks on tuples, using a LockTupleMode strength value.
     169              :  * This is more readable than having every caller translate it to lock.h's
     170              :  * LOCKMODE.
     171              :  */
     172              : #define LockTupleTuplock(rel, tup, mode) \
     173              :         LockTuple((rel), (tup), tupleLockExtraInfo[mode].hwlock)
     174              : #define UnlockTupleTuplock(rel, tup, mode) \
     175              :         UnlockTuple((rel), (tup), tupleLockExtraInfo[mode].hwlock)
     176              : #define ConditionalLockTupleTuplock(rel, tup, mode) \
     177              :         ConditionalLockTuple((rel), (tup), tupleLockExtraInfo[mode].hwlock)
     178              :

     188              :         int                     nitems;
     189              :         ItemPointerData *tids;
     190              : } XidHorizonPrefetchState;

     193              : /*
     194              :  * This table maps tuple lock strength values for each particular

     196              :  */
     197              : static const int MultiXactStatusLock[MaxMultiXactStatus + 1] =
     198              : {
     199              :         LockTupleKeyShare,                      /* ForKeyShare */
     200              :         LockTupleShare,                         /* ForShare */
     201              :         LockTupleNoKeyExclusive,        /* ForNoKeyUpdate */
     202              :         LockTupleExclusive,                     /* ForUpdate */
     203              :         LockTupleNoKeyExclusive,        /* NoKeyUpdate */
     204              :         LockTupleExclusive                      /* Update */

     206              : 
     207              : /* Get the LockTupleMode for a given MultiXactStatus */

     221         2754 : initscan(HeapScanDesc scan, ScanKey key, bool keep_startblock)

     223         2754 :         ParallelBlockTableScanDesc bpscan = NULL;
     224              :         bool            allow_strat;
     225              :         bool            allow_sync;
     226              : 
     227              :         /*
     228              :          * Determine the number of blocks we have to scan.
     229              :          *
     230              :          * It is sufficient to do this once at scan start, since any tuples added
     231              :          * while the scan is in progress will be invisible to my snapshot anyway.
     232              :          * (That is not true when using a non-MVCC snapshot.  However, we couldn't
     233              :          * guarantee to return tuples added after scan start anyway, since they
     234              :          * might go into pages we already scanned.  To guarantee consistent
     235              :          * results for a non-MVCC snapshot, the caller must hold some higher-level
     236              :          * lock that ensures the interesting tuple(s) won't change.)
     237              :          */
     238         2754 :         if (scan->rs_base.rs_parallel != NULL)
     239              :         {
     240            0 :                 bpscan = (ParallelBlockTableScanDesc) scan->rs_base.rs_parallel;

     242              :         }
     243              :         else
     244         2754 :                 scan->rs_nblocks = RelationGetNumberOfBlocks(scan->rs_base.rs_rd);
     245              : 
     246              :         /*
     247              :          * If the table is large relative to NBuffers, use a bulk-read access
     248              :          * strategy and enable synchronized scanning (see syncscan.c).  Although
     249              :          * the thresholds for these features could be different, we make them the
     250              :          * same so that there are only two behaviors to tune rather than four.
     251              :          * (However, some callers need to be able to disable one or both of these
     252              :          * behaviors, independently of the size of the table; also there is a GUC
     253              :          * variable that can disable synchronized scanning.)

     357              : /*
     358              :  * heapgetpage - subroutine for heapgettup()
     359              :  *
     360              :  * This routine reads and pins the specified page of the relation.
     361              :  * In page-at-a-time mode it performs additional work, namely determining
     362              :  * which tuples on the page are visible.
     363              :  */
     364              : void
     365        10109 : heapgetpage(TableScanDesc sscan, BlockNumber page)
     366              : {
     367        10109 :         HeapScanDesc scan = (HeapScanDesc) sscan;
     368              :         Buffer          buffer;
     369              :         Snapshot        snapshot;
     370              :         Page            dp;
     371              :         int                     lines;
     372              :         int                     ntup;
     373              :         OffsetNumber lineoff;
     374              :         ItemId          lpp;
     375              :         bool            all_visible;
     376              : 
     377              :         Assert(page < scan->rs_nblocks);
     378              : 
     379              :         /* release previous scan buffer, if any */
     380        10109 :         if (BufferIsValid(scan->rs_cbuf))
     381              :         {
     382         7990 :                 ReleaseBuffer(scan->rs_cbuf);
     383         7990 :                 scan->rs_cbuf = InvalidBuffer;
     384              :         }
     385              : 
     386              :         /*
     387              :          * Be sure to check for interrupts at least once per page.  Checks at
     388              :          * higher code levels won't be able to stop a seqscan that encounters many
     389              :          * pages' worth of consecutive dead tuples.
     390              :          */
     391        10109 :         CHECK_FOR_INTERRUPTS();
     392              : 
     393              :         /* read page using selected strategy */
     394        10109 :         scan->rs_cbuf = ReadBufferExtended(scan->rs_base.rs_rd, MAIN_FORKNUM, page,
     395              :                                                                            RBM_NORMAL, scan->rs_strategy);
     396        10109 :         scan->rs_cblock = page;
     397              : 
     398        10109 :         if (!(scan->rs_base.rs_flags & SO_ALLOW_PAGEMODE))
     399           44 :                 return;
     400              : 
     401        10065 :         buffer = scan->rs_cbuf;
     402        10065 :         snapshot = scan->rs_base.rs_snapshot;
     403              : 
     404              :         /*
     405              :          * Prune and repair fragmentation for the whole page, if possible.
     406              :          */
     407        10065 :         heap_page_prune_opt(scan->rs_base.rs_rd, buffer);
     408              : 
     409              :         /*
     410              :          * We must hold share lock on the buffer content while examining tuple
     411              :          * visibility.  Afterwards, however, the tuples we have found to be
     412              :          * visible are guaranteed good as long as we hold the buffer pin.
     413              :          */
     414        10065 :         LockBuffer(buffer, BUFFER_LOCK_SHARE);
     415              : 
     416        10065 :         dp = BufferGetPage(buffer);
     417        10065 :         TestForOldSnapshot(snapshot, scan->rs_base.rs_rd, dp);
     418        10065 :         lines = PageGetMaxOffsetNumber(dp);
     419        10065 :         ntup = 0;
     420              : 
     421              :         /*
     422              :          * If the all-visible flag indicates that all tuples on the page are
     423              :          * visible to everyone, we can skip the per-tuple visibility tests.
     424              :          *
     425              :          * Note: In hot standby, a tuple that's already visible to all
     426              :          * transactions in the master might still be invisible to a read-only
     427              :          * transaction in the standby. We partly handle this problem by tracking
     428              :          * the minimum xmin of visible tuples as the cut-off XID while marking a
     429              :          * page all-visible on master and WAL log that along with the visibility
     430              :          * map SET operation. In hot standby, we wait for (or abort) all
     431              :          * transactions that can potentially may not see one or more tuples on the
     432              :          * page. That's how index-only scans work fine in hot standby. A crucial
     433              :          * difference between index-only scans and heap scans is that the
     434              :          * index-only scan completely relies on the visibility map where as heap
     435              :          * scan looks at the page-level PD_ALL_VISIBLE flag. We are not sure if
     436              :          * the page-level flag can be trusted in the same way, because it might
     437              :          * get propagated somehow without being explicitly WAL-logged, e.g. via a
     438              :          * full page write. Until we can prove that beyond doubt, let's check each
     439              :          * tuple for visibility the hard way.
     440              :          */
     441        10065 :         all_visible = PageIsAllVisible(dp) && !snapshot->takenDuringRecovery;
     442              : 
     443        10065 :         for (lineoff = FirstOffsetNumber, lpp = PageGetItemId(dp, lineoff);
     444       511356 :                  lineoff <= lines;
     445       501291 :                  lineoff++, lpp++)
     446              :         {
     447       501291 :                 if (ItemIdIsNormal(lpp))
     448              :                 {
     449              :                         HeapTupleData loctup;
     450              :                         bool            valid;
     451              : 
     452       480243 :                         loctup.t_tableOid = RelationGetRelid(scan->rs_base.rs_rd);
     453       480243 :                         loctup.t_data = (HeapTupleHeader) PageGetItem((Page) dp, lpp);
     454       480243 :                         loctup.t_len = ItemIdGetLength(lpp);
     455       480243 :                         ItemPointerSet(&(loctup.t_self), page, lineoff);
     456              : 
     457       480243 :                         if (all_visible)
     458        47678 :                                 valid = true;
     459              :                         else
     460       432565 :                                 valid = HeapTupleSatisfiesVisibility(&loctup, snapshot, buffer);
     461              : 
     462       480243 :                         HeapCheckForSerializableConflictOut(valid, scan->rs_base.rs_rd,
     463              :                                                                                                 &loctup, buffer, snapshot);
     464              : 
     465       480243 :                         if (valid)
     466       479369 :                                 scan->rs_vistuples[ntup++] = lineoff;
     467              :                 }
     468              :         }
     469              : 
     470        10065 :         LockBuffer(buffer, BUFFER_LOCK_UNLOCK);
     471              : 
     472              :         Assert(ntup <= MaxHeapTuplesPerPage);
     473        10065 :         scan->rs_ntuples = ntup;
     474              : }
     475              : 
     476              : /* ----------------
     477              :  *              heapgettup - fetch next heap tuple
     478              :  *
     479              :  *              Initialize the scan if not already done; then advance to the next
     480              :  *              tuple as indicated by "dir"; return the next tuple in scan->rs_ctup,
     481              :  *              or set scan->rs_ctup.t_data = NULL if no more tuples.
     482              :  *
     483              :  * dir == NoMovementScanDirection means "re-fetch the tuple indicated
     484              :  * by scan->rs_ctup".
     485              :  *
     486              :  * Note: the reason nkeys/key are passed separately, even though they are
     487              :  * kept in the scan descriptor, is that the caller may not want us to check
     488              :  * the scankeys.
     489              :  *
     490              :  * Note: when we fall off the end of the scan in either direction, we
     491              :  * reset rs_inited.  This means that a further request with the same
     492              :  * scan direction will restart the scan, which is a bit odd, but a
     493              :  * request with the opposite scan direction will start a fresh scan
     494              :  * in the proper direction.  The latter is required behavior for cursors,
     495              :  * while the former case is generally undefined behavior in Postgres

    1191              :         /*
    1192              :          * For seqscan and sample scans in a serializable transaction, acquire a
    1193              :          * predicate lock on the entire relation. This is required not only to
    1194              :          * lock all the matching tuples, but also to conflict with new insertions
    1195              :          * into the table. In an indexscan, we take page locks on the index pages
    1196              :          * covering the range specified in the scan qual, but in a heap scan there
    1197              :          * is nothing more fine-grained to lock. A bitmap scan is a different
    1198              :          * story, there we have already scanned the index and locked the index

    1200              :          * any matching heap tuples. For sample scan we could optimize the locking

    1368              : /*
    1369              :  *      heap_fetch              - retrieve tuple with given tid
    1370              :  *
    1371              :  * On entry, tuple->t_self is the TID to fetch.  We pin the buffer holding
    1372              :  * the tuple, fill in the remaining fields of *tuple, and check the tuple
    1373              :  * against the specified snapshot.
    1374              :  *
    1375              :  * If successful (tuple found and passes snapshot time qual), then *userbuf
    1376              :  * is set to the buffer holding the tuple and true is returned.  The caller
    1377              :  * must unpin the buffer when done with the tuple.
    1378              :  *
    1379              :  * If the tuple is not found (ie, item number references a deleted slot),
    1380              :  * then tuple->t_data is set to NULL, *userbuf is set to InvalidBuffer,
    1381              :  * and false is returned.
    1382              :  *
    1383              :  * If the tuple is found but fails the time qual check, then false is returned
    1384              :  * and *userbuf is set to InvalidBuffer, but tuple->t_data is left pointing
    1385              :  * to the tuple.  (Note that it is unsafe to dereference tuple->t_data in
    1386              :  * this case, but callers might choose to test it for NULL-ness.)
    1387              :  *
    1388              :  * heap_fetch does not follow HOT chains: only the exact TID requested will
    1389              :  * be fetched.
    1390              :  *
    1391              :  * It is somewhat inconsistent that we ereport() on invalid block number but
    1392              :  * return false on invalid item number.  There are a couple of reasons though.
    1393              :  * One is that the caller can relatively easily check the block number for
    1394              :  * validity, but cannot check the item number without reading the page
    1395              :  * himself.  Another is that when we are following a t_ctid link, we can be
    1396              :  * reasonably confident that the page number is valid (since VACUUM shouldn't
    1397              :  * truncate off the destination page without having killed the referencing
    1398              :  * tuple first), but the item number might well not be good.
    1399              :  */
    1400              : bool
    1401           22 : heap_fetch(Relation relation,
    1402              :                    Snapshot snapshot,
    1403              :                    HeapTuple tuple,

    1407              : }

    1414              :  * buffer ID in *userbuf, keeping the buffer pin, just as if it had passed
    1415              :  * the snapshot.  (The function result is still "false" though.)
    1416              :  * If keep_buf is false then this behaves identically to heap_fetch().

    1518              : /*
    1519              :  *      heap_hot_search_buffer  - search HOT chain for tuple satisfying snapshot
    1520              :  *
    1521              :  * On entry, *tid is the TID of a tuple (either a simple tuple, or the root
    1522              :  * of a HOT chain), and buffer is the buffer holding this tuple.  We search
    1523              :  * for the first chain member satisfying the given snapshot.  If one is
    1524              :  * found, we update *tid to reference that tuple's offset number, and
    1525              :  * return true.  If no match, return false without modifying *tid.
    1526              :  *
    1527              :  * heapTuple is a caller-supplied buffer.  When a match is found, we return
    1528              :  * the tuple here, in addition to updating *tid.  If no match is found, the
    1529              :  * contents of this buffer on return are undefined.
    1530              :  *
    1531              :  * If all_dead is not NULL, we check non-visible tuples to see if they are
    1532              :  * globally dead; *all_dead is set true if all members of the HOT chain
    1533              :  * are vacuumable, false if not.
    1534              :  *
    1535              :  * Unlike heap_fetch, the caller must already have pin and (at least) share
    1536              :  * lock on the buffer; it is still pinned/locked at exit.
    1537              :  */
    1538              : bool
    1539        50210 : heap_hot_search_buffer(ItemPointer tid, Relation relation, Buffer buffer,
    1540              :                                            Snapshot snapshot, HeapTuple heapTuple,
    1541              :                                            bool *all_dead, bool first_call)
    1542              : {
    1543        50210 :         Page            dp = (Page) BufferGetPage(buffer);
    1544        50210 :         TransactionId prev_xmax = InvalidTransactionId;
    1545              :         BlockNumber blkno;
    1546              :         OffsetNumber offnum;
    1547              :         bool            at_chain_start;
    1548              :         bool            valid;
    1549              :         bool            skip;
    1550              : 
    1551              :         /* If this is not the first call, previous call returned a (live!) tuple */
    1552        50210 :         if (all_dead)
    1553        50152 :                 *all_dead = first_call;
    1554              :

    1790              : /*
    1791              :  * UpdateXmaxHintBits - update tuple hint bits after xmax transaction ends
    1792              :  *
    1793              :  * This is called after we have waited for the XMAX transaction to terminate.

    1795              :  * be set on exit.  If the transaction committed, we set the XMAX_COMMITTED
    1796              :  * hint bit if possible --- but beware that that may not yet be possible,
    1797              :  * if the transaction committed asynchronously.
    1798              :  *

    1800              :  * even if it commits.
    1801              :  *
    1802              :  * Hence callers should look only at XMAX_INVALID.
    1803              :  *
    1804              :  * Note this is not allowed for tuples whose xmax is a multixact.

    1806              : static void
    1807            0 : UpdateXmaxHintBits(HeapTupleHeader tuple, Buffer buffer, TransactionId xid)

    1815            0 :                         TransactionIdDidCommit(xid))

    1817              :                                                                  xid);

    1819            0 :                         HeapTupleSetHintBits(tuple, buffer, HEAP_XMAX_INVALID,
    1820              :                                                                  InvalidTransactionId);
    1821              :         }
    1822            0 : }

    1863              : /*
    1864              :  *      heap_insert             - insert tuple into a heap
    1865              :  *
    1866              :  * The new tuple is stamped with current transaction ID and the specified
    1867              :  * command ID.
    1868              :  *
    1869              :  * See table_tuple_insert for comments about most of the input flags, except
    1870              :  * that this routine directly takes a tuple rather than a slot.
    1871              :  *
    1872              :  * There's corresponding HEAP_INSERT_ options to all the TABLE_INSERT_
    1873              :  * options, and there additionally is HEAP_INSERT_SPECULATIVE which is used to
    1874              :  * implement table_tuple_insert_speculative().
    1875              :  *
    1876              :  * On return the header fields of *tup are updated to match the stored tuple;
    1877              :  * in particular tup->t_self receives the actual TID where the tuple was
    1878              :  * stored.  But note that any toasting of fields within the tuple data is NOT
    1879              :  * reflected into *tup.
    1880              :  */

    1882        34217 : heap_insert(Relation relation, HeapTuple tup, CommandId cid,
    1883              :                         int options, BulkInsertState bistate)
    1884              : {
    1885        34217 :         TransactionId xid = GetCurrentTransactionId();
    1886              :         HeapTuple       heaptup;
    1887              :         Buffer          buffer;
    1888        34217 :         Buffer          vmbuffer = InvalidBuffer;
    1889        34217 :         bool            all_visible_cleared = false;
    1890              : 
    1891              :         /* Cheap, simplistic check that the tuple matches the rel's rowtype. */
    1892              :         Assert(HeapTupleHeaderGetNatts(tup->t_data) <=
    1893              :                    RelationGetNumberOfAttributes(relation));
    1894              : 
    1895              :         /*

    1897              :          *

    1899              :          * into the relation; tup is the caller's original untoasted data.
    1900              :          */
    1901        34217 :         heaptup = heap_prepare_insert(relation, tup, xid, cid, options);
    1902              : 
    1903              :         /*
    1904              :          * Find buffer to insert this tuple into.  If the page is all visible,
    1905              :          * this will also pin the requisite visibility map page.
    1906              :          */

    1908              :                                                                            InvalidBuffer, options, bistate,
    1909              :                                                                            &vmbuffer, NULL);
    1910              : 
    1911              :         /*
    1912              :          * We're about to do the actual insert -- but check for conflict first, to
    1913              :          * avoid possibly having to roll back work we've just done.
    1914              :          *
    1915              :          * This is safe without a recheck as long as there is no possibility of
    1916              :          * another process scanning the page between this check and the insert
    1917              :          * being visible to the scan (i.e., an exclusive buffer content lock is
    1918              :          * continuously held from this point until the tuple insert is visible).
    1919              :          *
    1920              :          * For a heap insert, we only need to check for table-level SSI locks. Our
    1921              :          * new tuple can't possibly conflict with existing tuple locks, and heap
    1922              :          * page locks are only consolidated versions of tuple locks; they do not
    1923              :          * lock "gaps" as index page locks do.  So we don't need to specify a
    1924              :          * buffer when making the call, which makes for a faster check.
    1925              :          */
    1926        34217 :         CheckForSerializableConflictIn(relation, NULL, InvalidBlockNumber);
    1927              : 
    1928              :         /* NO EREPORT(ERROR) from here till changes are logged */
    1929        34217 :         START_CRIT_SECTION();
    1930              : 
    1931        34217 :         RelationPutHeapTuple(relation, buffer, heaptup,
    1932        34217 :                                                  (options & HEAP_INSERT_SPECULATIVE) != 0);
    1933              : 
    1934        34217 :         if (PageIsAllVisible(BufferGetPage(buffer)))
    1935              :         {

    1941              :         }
    1942              : 
    1943              :         /*
    1944              :          * XXX Should we set PageSetPrunable on this page ?

    1948              :          * for aborts, if no other tuple in this page is UPDATEd/DELETEd, the
    1949              :          * aborted tuple will never be pruned until next vacuum is triggered.

    1952              :          */

    1954        34217 :         MarkBufferDirty(buffer);

    1956              :         /* XLOG stuff */

    1965              : 
    1966              :                 /*

    1972              : 
    1973              :                 /*

    1979          614 :                         PageGetMaxOffsetNumber(page) == FirstOffsetNumber)

    1982          613 :                         bufflags |= REGBUF_WILL_INIT;
    1983              :                 }
    1984              : 
    1985        34213 :                 xlrec.offnum = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(&heaptup->t_self);

    1991              :                 Assert(ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(&heaptup->t_self) == BufferGetBlockNumber(buffer));
    1992              : 
    1993              :                 /*

    2003              :                 }
    2004              : 
    2005        34213 :                 XLogBeginInsert();
    2006        34213 :                 XLogRegisterData((char *) &xlrec, SizeOfHeapInsert);
    2007              :

    2023              :

    2032        34217 :         END_CRIT_SECTION();

    2034        34217 :         UnlockReleaseBuffer(buffer);

    2037              :

    2060              : /*
    2061              :  * Subroutine for heap_insert(). Prepares a tuple for insertion. This sets the
    2062              :  * tuple header fields and toasts the tuple if necessary.  Returns a toasted
    2063              :  * version of the tuple if it was toasted, or the original tuple if not. Note
    2064              :  * that in any case, the header fields are also set in the original tuple.
    2065              :  */

    2072              :          * performed in workers. We have the infrastructure to allow parallel
    2073              :          * inserts in general except for the cases where inserts generate a new
    2074              :          * CommandId (eg. inserts into a table having a foreign key column).
    2075              :          */
    2076        34929 :         if (IsParallelWorker())
    2077            0 :                 ereport(ERROR,
    2078              :                                 (errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_TRANSACTION_STATE),
    2079              :                                  errmsg("cannot insert tuples in a parallel worker")));

    2086            5 :                 HeapTupleHeaderSetXminFrozen(tup->t_data);
    2087              : 
    2088        34929 :         HeapTupleHeaderSetCmin(tup->t_data, cid);

    2091              : 
    2092              :         /*

    2107              : }

    2109              : /*
    2110              :  *      heap_multi_insert       - insert multiple tuple into a heap
    2111              :  *
    2112              :  * This is like heap_insert(), but inserts multiple tuples in one operation.
    2113              :  * That's faster than calling heap_insert() in a loop, because when multiple
    2114              :  * tuples can be inserted on a single page, we can write just a single WAL
    2115              :  * record covering all of them, and only need to lock/unlock the page once.
    2116              :  *

    2118              :  * temporary context before calling this, if that's a problem.
    2119              :  */

    2124            1 :         TransactionId xid = GetCurrentTransactionId();

    2155              :         /*
    2156              :          * We're about to do the actual inserts -- but check for conflict first,

    2159              :          *
    2160              :          * A check here does not definitively prevent a serialization anomaly;
    2161              :          * that check MUST be done at least past the point of acquiring an
    2162              :          * exclusive buffer content lock on every buffer that will be affected,
    2163              :          * and MAY be done after all inserts are reflected in the buffers and
    2164              :          * those locks are released; otherwise there is a race condition.  Since
    2165              :          * multiple buffers can be locked and unlocked in the loop below, and it

    2171              :          *

    2173              :          * new tuples can't possibly conflict with existing tuple locks, and heap
    2174              :          * page locks are only consolidated versions of tuple locks; they do not
    2175              :          * lock "gaps" as index page locks do.  So we don't need to specify a

    2398              : /*
    2399              :  *      simple_heap_insert - insert a tuple

    2413              : /*

    2450              : 
    2451            0 :         return false;

    2454              : /*
    2455              :  *      heap_delete - delete a tuple
    2456              :  *

    2462              :  * only for TM_SelfModified, since we cannot obtain cmax from a combocid

    2466         5492 : heap_delete(Relation relation, ItemPointer tid,
    2467              :                         CommandId cid, Snapshot crosscheck, bool wait,
    2468              :                         TM_FailureData *tmfd, bool changingPart)
    2469              : {
    2470              :         TM_Result       result;

    2474              :         Page            page;
    2475              :         BlockNumber block;
    2476              :         Buffer          buffer;
    2477         5492 :         Buffer          vmbuffer = InvalidBuffer;
    2478              :         TransactionId new_xmax;
    2479              :         uint16          new_infomask,
    2480              :                                 new_infomask2;
    2481         5492 :         bool            have_tuple_lock = false;
    2482              :         bool            iscombo;
    2483         5492 :         bool            all_visible_cleared = false;

    2486              : 
    2487              :         Assert(ItemPointerIsValid(tid));
    2488              : 
    2489              :         /*
    2490              :          * Forbid this during a parallel operation, lest it allocate a combocid.

    2493              :          */
    2494         5492 :         if (IsInParallelMode())
    2495            0 :                 ereport(ERROR,
    2496              :                                 (errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_TRANSACTION_STATE),
    2497              :                                  errmsg("cannot delete tuples during a parallel operation")));
    2498              : 
    2499         5492 :         block = ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(tid);
    2500         5492 :         buffer = ReadBuffer(relation, block);
    2501         5492 :         page = BufferGetPage(buffer);
    2502              : 
    2503              :         /*
    2504              :          * Before locking the buffer, pin the visibility map page if it appears to

    2507              :          * the lock.

    2509         5492 :         if (PageIsAllVisible(page))
    2510            3 :                 visibilitymap_pin(relation, block, &vmbuffer);
    2511              :

    2875              : /*
    2876              :  *      simple_heap_delete - delete a tuple
    2877              :  *
    2878              :  * This routine may be used to delete a tuple when concurrent updates of

    2900         5198 :                 case TM_Ok:
    2901              :                         /* done successfully */

    2903              : 
    2904            0 :                 case TM_Updated:

    2907              : 
    2908            0 :                 case TM_Deleted:

    2910              :                         break;

    2918              : /*

    2993              :         /*

    2998              :          * list before obtaining buffer lock --- in the worst case, if we are
    2999              :          * doing an update on one of the relevant system catalogs, we could
    3000              :          * deadlock if we try to fetch the list later.  In any case, the relcache
    3001              :          * caches the data so this is usually pretty cheap.

    3062              :          * hold a snapshot that ensures LP_NORMAL.  Hence, the choice between
    3063              :          * TM_Updated and TM_Deleted affects only the wording of error messages.

    3624              :                  * first".  To implement this, we must do RelationGetBufferForTuple

    3880              : 
    3881              : #ifdef USE_ASSERT_CHECKING
    3882              : /*
    3883              :  * Confirm adequate lock held during heap_update(), per rules from
    3884              :  * README.tuplock section "Locking to write inplace-updated tables".
    3885              :  */
    3886              : static void
    3887              : check_lock_if_inplace_updateable_rel(Relation relation,

    3889              :                                                                          HeapTuple newtup)

    3891              :         /* LOCKTAG_TUPLE acceptable for any catalog */

    3893              :         {
    3894              :                 case RelationRelationId:
    3895              :                 case DatabaseRelationId:

    3897              :                                 LOCKTAG         tuptag;
    3898              : 
    3899              :                                 SET_LOCKTAG_TUPLE(tuptag,

    3912              : 
    3913              :         switch (RelationGetRelid(relation))
    3914              :         {
    3915              :                 case RelationRelationId:
    3916              :                         {

    3918              :                                 Form_pg_class classForm = (Form_pg_class) GETSTRUCT(newtup);

    3922              : 
    3923              :                                 if (IsSharedRelation(relid))
    3924              :                                         dbid = InvalidOid;

    3926              :                                         dbid = MyDatabaseId;

    3962              :         }

    4214              : /*
    4215              :  *      heap_lock_tuple - lock a tuple in shared or exclusive mode

    4380              :

    4386            0 :                                 switch (mode)

    4388            0 :                                         case LockTupleKeyShare:

    4391              :                                                            HEAP_XMAX_IS_EXCL_LOCKED(infomask));
    4392            0 :                                                 result = TM_Ok;
    4393            0 :                                                 goto out_unlocked;
    4394            0 :                                         case LockTupleShare:
    4395            0 :                                                 if (HEAP_XMAX_IS_SHR_LOCKED(infomask) ||

    4421              :                 /*

    4427            0 :                 if (mode == LockTupleKeyShare)
    4428              :                 {
    4429              :                         /*
    4430              :                          * If we're requesting KeyShare, and there's no update present, we
    4431              :                          * don't need to wait.  Even if there is an update, we can still

    4433              :                          *
    4434              :                          * However, if there are updates, we need to walk the update chain
    4435              :                          * to mark future versions of the row as locked, too.  That way,
    4436              :                          * if somebody deletes that future version, we're protected
    4437              :                          * against the key going away.  This locking of future versions

    4439              :                          * deleting a key; or it could return a value to the effect that

    4442              :                          * prone to deadlocks.

    4445              :                          * the buffer.  For it to change while we're not looking, the

    4872              :         /*

    4876              :          * MultiXactId that may never have been seen on disk before, and we need
    4877              :          * to make sure that there are XLOG entries covering those ID numbers.
    4878              :          * Else the same IDs might be re-used after a crash, which would be
    4879              :          * disastrous if this page made it to disk before the crash.  Essentially
    4880              :          * we have to enforce the WAL log-before-data rule even in this case.

    4882              :          * entries for everything anyway.)

    4932              : /*

    4957            0 :                 case LockWaitSkip:

    4962            0 :                 case LockWaitError:

    4969              :         }

    4971              : 
    4972            0 :         return true;

    5259              : /*
    5260              :  * Subroutine for heap_lock_updated_tuple_rec.
    5261              :  *
    5262              :  * Given a hypothetical multixact status held by the transaction identified
    5263              :  * with the given xid, does the current transaction need to wait, fail, or can
    5264              :  * it continue if it wanted to acquire a lock of the given mode?  "needwait"
    5265              :  * is set to true if waiting is necessary; if it can continue, then TM_Ok is
    5266              :  * returned.  If the lock is already held by the current transaction, return
    5267              :  * TM_SelfModified.  In case of a conflict with another transaction, a
    5268              :  * different HeapTupleSatisfiesUpdate return code is returned.
    5269              :  *
    5270              :  * The held status is said to be hypothetical because it might correspond to a
    5271              :  * lock held by a single Xid, i.e. not a real MultiXactId; we express it this
    5272              :  * way for simplicity of API.
    5273              :  */
    5274              : static TM_Result
    5275            0 : test_lockmode_for_conflict(MultiXactStatus status, TransactionId xid,
    5276              :                                                    LockTupleMode mode, HeapTuple tup,
    5277              :                                                    bool *needwait)
    5278              : {
    5279              :         MultiXactStatus wantedstatus;
    5280              : 
    5281            0 :         *needwait = false;
    5282            0 :         wantedstatus = get_mxact_status_for_lock(mode, false);
    5283              : 
    5284              :         /*
    5285              :          * Note: we *must* check TransactionIdIsInProgress before
    5286              :          * TransactionIdDidAbort/Commit; see comment at top of heapam_visibility.c
    5287              :          * for an explanation.
    5288              :          */
    5289            0 :         if (TransactionIdIsCurrentTransactionId(xid))
    5290              :         {
    5291              :                 /*
    5292              :                  * The tuple has already been locked by our own transaction.  This is
    5293              :                  * very rare but can happen if multiple transactions are trying to
    5294              :                  * lock an ancient version of the same tuple.
    5295              :                  */
    5296            0 :                 return TM_SelfModified;
    5297              :         }
    5298            0 :         else if (TransactionIdIsInProgress(xid))
    5299              :         {
    5300              :                 /*
    5301              :                  * If the locking transaction is running, what we do depends on
    5302              :                  * whether the lock modes conflict: if they do, then we must wait for
    5303              :                  * it to finish; otherwise we can fall through to lock this tuple

    5305              :                  */
    5306            0 :                 if (DoLockModesConflict(LOCKMODE_from_mxstatus(status),

    5309            0 :                         *needwait = true;
    5310              :                 }
    5311              : 
    5312              :                 /*

    5314              :                  * otherwise, this value signals to caller that it's okay to proceed.
    5315              :                  */
    5316            0 :                 return TM_Ok;
    5317              :         }
    5318            0 :         else if (TransactionIdDidAbort(xid))

    5320            0 :         else if (TransactionIdDidCommit(xid))
    5321              :         {
    5322              :                 /*
    5323              :                  * The other transaction committed.  If it was only a locker, then the
    5324              :                  * lock is completely gone now and we can return success; but if it
    5325              :                  * was an update, then what we do depends on whether the two lock
    5326              :                  * modes conflict.  If they conflict, then we must report error to
    5327              :                  * caller. But if they don't, we can fall through to allow the current
    5328              :                  * transaction to lock the tuple.
    5329              :                  *
    5330              :                  * Note: the reason we worry about ISUPDATE here is because as soon as
    5331              :                  * a transaction ends, all its locks are gone and meaningless, and
    5332              :                  * thus we can ignore them; whereas its updates persist.  In the
    5333              :                  * TransactionIdIsInProgress case, above, we don't need to check

    5688              : 
    5689              : /*
    5690              :  * heap_lock_updated_tuple
    5691              :  *              Follow update chain when locking an updated tuple, acquiring locks (row
    5692              :  *              marks) on the updated versions.
    5693              :  *
    5694              :  * The initial tuple is assumed to be already locked.
    5695              :  *
    5696              :  * This function doesn't check visibility, it just unconditionally marks the
    5697              :  * tuple(s) as locked.  If any tuple in the updated chain is being deleted
    5698              :  * concurrently (or updated with the key being modified), sleep until the
    5699              :  * transaction doing it is finished.
    5700              :  *
    5701              :  * Note that we don't acquire heavyweight tuple locks on the tuples we walk
    5702              :  * when we have to wait for other transactions to release them, as opposed to
    5703              :  * what heap_lock_tuple does.  The reason is that having more than one
    5704              :  * transaction walking the chain is probably uncommon enough that risk of
    5705              :  * starvation is not likely: one of the preconditions for being here is that
    5706              :  * the snapshot in use predates the update that created this tuple (because we
    5707              :  * started at an earlier version of the tuple), but at the same time such a
    5708              :  * transaction cannot be using repeatable read or serializable isolation
    5709              :  * levels, because that would lead to a serializability failure.
    5710              :  */
    5711              : static TM_Result
    5712            0 : heap_lock_updated_tuple(Relation rel, HeapTuple tuple, ItemPointer ctid,
    5713              :                                                 TransactionId xid, LockTupleMode mode)
    5714              : {
    5715              :         /*
    5716              :          * If the tuple has not been updated, or has moved into another partition
    5717              :          * (effectively a delete) stop here.
    5718              :          */
    5719            0 :         if (!HeapTupleHeaderIndicatesMovedPartitions(tuple->t_data) &&
    5720            0 :                 !ItemPointerEquals(&tuple->t_self, ctid))
    5721              :         {
    5722              :                 /*
    5723              :                  * If this is the first possibly-multixact-able operation in the
    5724              :                  * current transaction, set my per-backend OldestMemberMXactId
    5725              :                  * setting. We can be certain that the transaction will never become a
    5726              :                  * member of any older MultiXactIds than that.  (We have to do this
    5727              :                  * even if we end up just using our own TransactionId below, since
    5728              :                  * some other backend could incorporate our XID into a MultiXact
    5729              :                  * immediately afterwards.)
    5730              :                  */
    5731            0 :                 MultiXactIdSetOldestMember();
    5732              : 
    5733            0 :                 return heap_lock_updated_tuple_rec(rel, ctid, xid, mode);
    5734              :         }
    5735              : 
    5736              :         /* nothing to lock */
    5737            0 :         return TM_Ok;
    5738              : }
    5739              : 
    5740              : /*
    5741              :  *      heap_finish_speculative - mark speculative insertion as successful
    5742              :  *
    5743              :  * To successfully finish a speculative insertion we have to clear speculative
    5744              :  * token from tuple.  To do so the t_ctid field, which will contain a
    5745              :  * speculative token value, is modified in place to point to the tuple itself,
    5746              :  * which is characteristic of a newly inserted ordinary tuple.
    5747              :  *
    5748              :  * NB: It is not ok to commit without either finishing or aborting a
    5749              :  * speculative insertion.  We could treat speculative tuples of committed
    5750              :  * transactions implicitly as completed, but then we would have to be prepared
    5751              :  * to deal with speculative tokens on committed tuples.  That wouldn't be
    5752              :  * difficult - no-one looks at the ctid field of a tuple with invalid xmax -
    5753              :  * but clearing the token at completion isn't very expensive either.
    5754              :  * An explicit confirmation WAL record also makes logical decoding simpler.
    5755              :  */
    5756              : void
    5757            0 : heap_finish_speculative(Relation relation, ItemPointer tid)
    5758              : {
    5759              :         Buffer          buffer;
    5760              :         Page            page;
    5761              :         OffsetNumber offnum;
    5762            0 :         ItemId          lp = NULL;
    5763              :         HeapTupleHeader htup;
    5764              : 
    5765            0 :         buffer = ReadBuffer(relation, ItemPointerGetBlockNumber(tid));
    5766            0 :         LockBuffer(buffer, BUFFER_LOCK_EXCLUSIVE);
    5767            0 :         page = (Page) BufferGetPage(buffer);
    5768              : 
    5769            0 :         offnum = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(tid);
    5770            0 :         if (PageGetMaxOffsetNumber(page) >= offnum)
    5771            0 :                 lp = PageGetItemId(page, offnum);
    5772              : 
    5773            0 :         if (PageGetMaxOffsetNumber(page) < offnum || !ItemIdIsNormal(lp))
    5774            0 :                 elog(ERROR, "invalid lp");
    5775              : 
    5776            0 :         htup = (HeapTupleHeader) PageGetItem(page, lp);
    5777              : 
    5778              :         /* SpecTokenOffsetNumber should be distinguishable from any real offset */
    5779              :         StaticAssertStmt(MaxOffsetNumber < SpecTokenOffsetNumber,
    5780              :                                          "invalid speculative token constant");
    5781              : 
    5782              :         /* NO EREPORT(ERROR) from here till changes are logged */
    5783            0 :         START_CRIT_SECTION();
    5784              : 
    5785              :         Assert(HeapTupleHeaderIsSpeculative(htup));
    5786              : 
    5787            0 :         MarkBufferDirty(buffer);
    5788              : 
    5789              :         /*
    5790              :          * Replace the speculative insertion token with a real t_ctid, pointing to
    5791              :          * itself like it does on regular tuples.
    5792              :          */
    5793            0 :         htup->t_ctid = *tid;
    5794              : 
    5795              :         /* XLOG stuff */
    5796            0 :         if (RelationNeedsWAL(relation))
    5797              :         {
    5798              :                 xl_heap_confirm xlrec;
    5799              :                 XLogRecPtr      recptr;
    5800              : 
    5801            0 :                 xlrec.offnum = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(tid);
    5802              : 
    5803            0 :                 XLogBeginInsert();
    5804              : 
    5805              :                 /* We want the same filtering on this as on a plain insert */
    5806            0 :                 XLogSetRecordFlags(XLOG_INCLUDE_ORIGIN);
    5807              : 
    5808            0 :                 XLogRegisterData((char *) &xlrec, SizeOfHeapConfirm);
    5809            0 :                 XLogRegisterBuffer(0, buffer, REGBUF_STANDARD);
    5810              : 
    5811            0 :                 recptr = XLogInsert(RM_HEAP_ID, XLOG_HEAP_CONFIRM);
    5812              : 
    5813            0 :                 PageSetLSN(page, recptr);
    5814              :         }
    5815              : 
    5816            0 :         END_CRIT_SECTION();
    5817              : 
    5818            0 :         UnlockReleaseBuffer(buffer);
    5819            0 : }
    5820              : 
    5821              : /*
    5822              :  *      heap_abort_speculative - kill a speculatively inserted tuple
    5823              :  *
    5824              :  * Marks a tuple that was speculatively inserted in the same command as dead,
    5825              :  * by setting its xmin as invalid.  That makes it immediately appear as dead
    5826              :  * to all transactions, including our own.  In particular, it makes
    5827              :  * HeapTupleSatisfiesDirty() regard the tuple as dead, so that another backend
    5828              :  * inserting a duplicate key value won't unnecessarily wait for our whole
    5829              :  * transaction to finish (it'll just wait for our speculative insertion to
    5830              :  * finish).
    5831              :  *
    5832              :  * Killing the tuple prevents "unprincipled deadlocks", which are deadlocks
    5833              :  * that arise due to a mutual dependency that is not user visible.  By
    5834              :  * definition, unprincipled deadlocks cannot be prevented by the user
    5835              :  * reordering lock acquisition in client code, because the implementation level
    5836              :  * lock acquisitions are not under the user's direct control.  If speculative
    5837              :  * inserters did not take this precaution, then under high concurrency they
    5838              :  * could deadlock with each other, which would not be acceptable.
    5839              :  *
    5840              :  * This is somewhat redundant with heap_delete, but we prefer to have a
    5841              :  * dedicated routine with stripped down requirements.  Note that this is also
    5842              :  * used to delete the TOAST tuples created during speculative insertion.
    5843              :  *

    5845              :  * confirmation records.
    5846              :  */

    5981              : /*
    5982              :  * heap_inplace_lock - protect inplace update from concurrent heap_update()
    5983              :  *
    5984              :  * Evaluate whether the tuple's state is compatible with a no-key update.
    5985              :  * Current transaction rowmarks are fine, as is KEY SHARE from any
    5986              :  * transaction.  If compatible, return true with the buffer exclusive-locked,
    5987              :  * and the caller must release that by calling
    5988              :  * heap_inplace_update_and_unlock(), calling heap_inplace_unlock(), or raising
    5989              :  * an error.  Otherwise, call release_callback(arg), wait for blocking
    5990              :  * transactions to end, and return false.
    5991              :  *
    5992              :  * Since this is intended for system catalogs and SERIALIZABLE doesn't cover
    5993              :  * DDL, this doesn't guarantee any particular predicate locking.
    5994              :  *
    5995              :  * One could modify this to return true for tuples with delete in progress,
    5996              :  * All inplace updaters take a lock that conflicts with DROP.  If explicit
    5997              :  * "DELETE FROM pg_class" is in progress, we'll wait for it like we would an
    5998              :  * update.
    5999              :  *
    6000              :  * Readers of inplace-updated fields expect changes to those fields are
    6001              :  * durable.  For example, vac_truncate_clog() reads datfrozenxid from
    6002              :  * pg_database tuples via catalog snapshots.  A future snapshot must not
    6003              :  * return a lower datfrozenxid for the same database OID (lower in the
    6004              :  * FullTransactionIdPrecedes() sense).  We achieve that since no update of a
    6005              :  * tuple can start while we hold a lock on its buffer.  In cases like
    6006              :  * BEGIN;GRANT;CREATE INDEX;COMMIT we're inplace-updating a tuple visible only
    6007              :  * to this transaction.  ROLLBACK then is one case where it's okay to lose
    6008              :  * inplace updates.  (Restoring relhasindex=false on ROLLBACK is fine, since
    6009              :  * any concurrent CREATE INDEX would have blocked, then inplace-updated the
    6010              :  * committed tuple.)
    6011              :  *
    6012              :  * In principle, we could avoid waiting by overwriting every tuple in the
    6013              :  * updated tuple chain.  Reader expectations permit updating a tuple only if
    6014              :  * it's aborted, is the tail of the chain, or we already updated the tuple
    6015              :  * referenced in its t_ctid.  Hence, we would need to overwrite the tuples in
    6016              :  * order from tail to head.  That would imply either (a) mutating all tuples
    6017              :  * in one critical section or (b) accepting a chance of partial completion.
    6018              :  * Partial completion of a relfrozenxid update would have the weird
    6019              :  * consequence that the table's next VACUUM could see the table's relfrozenxid
    6020              :  * move forward between vacuum_get_cutoffs() and finishing.
    6021              :  */

    6617              : /*
    6618              :  * heap_prepare_freeze_tuple
    6619              :  *
    6620              :  * Check to see whether any of the XID fields of a tuple (xmin, xmax, xvac)
    6621              :  * are older than the specified cutoff XID and cutoff MultiXactId.  If so,
    6622              :  * setup enough state (in the *frz output argument) to later execute and
    6623              :  * WAL-log what we would need to do, and return true.  Return false if nothing
    6624              :  * is to be changed.  In addition, set *totally_frozen_p to true if the tuple
    6625              :  * will be totally frozen after these operations are performed and false if
    6626              :  * more freezing will eventually be required.
    6627              :  *
    6628              :  * Caller is responsible for setting the offset field, if appropriate.
    6629              :  *
    6630              :  * It is assumed that the caller has checked the tuple with
    6631              :  * HeapTupleSatisfiesVacuum() and determined that it is not HEAPTUPLE_DEAD
    6632              :  * (else we should be removing the tuple, not freezing it).
    6633              :  *
    6634              :  * NB: cutoff_xid *must* be <= the current global xmin, to ensure that any
    6635              :  * XID older than it could neither be running nor seen as running by any
    6636              :  * open transaction.  This ensures that the replacement will not change
    6637              :  * anyone's idea of the tuple state.
    6638              :  * Similarly, cutoff_multi must be less than or equal to the smallest
    6639              :  * MultiXactId used by any transaction currently open.
    6640              :  *
    6641              :  * If the tuple is in a shared buffer, caller must hold an exclusive lock on
    6642              :  * that buffer.
    6643              :  *
    6644              :  * NB: It is not enough to set hint bits to indicate something is
    6645              :  * committed/invalid -- they might not be set on a standby, or after crash
    6646              :  * recovery.  We really need to remove old xids.
    6647              :  */

    6649        25769 : heap_prepare_freeze_tuple(HeapTupleHeader tuple,
    6650              :                                                   TransactionId relfrozenxid, TransactionId relminmxid,

    6652              :                                                   xl_heap_freeze_tuple *frz, bool *totally_frozen_p)
    6653              : {

    6665              :         /*
    6666              :          * Process xmin.  xmin_frozen has two slightly different meanings: in the

    6668              :          * already a permanent value), while in the block below it is set true to
    6669              :          * mean "xmin won't need freezing after what we do to it here" (false
    6670              :          * otherwise).  In both cases we're allowed to set totally_frozen, as far
    6671              :          * as xmin is concerned.

    6679            0 :                         ereport(ERROR,

    6697              : 
    6698              :         /*
    6699              :          * Process xmax.  To thoroughly examine the current Xmax value we need to

    6702              :          * freezing, too.  Also, if a multi needs freezing, we cannot simply take

    6717              :

    6733            0 :                         changed = true;
    6734              :                 }
    6735            0 :                 else if (flags & FRM_RETURN_IS_MULTI)
    6736              :                 {
    6737              :                         uint16          newbits;

    6739              : 
    6740              :                         /*

    6745              :                          */
    6746            0 :                         frz->t_infomask &= ~HEAP_XMAX_BITS;

    6751              :

    6753              : 
    6754            0 :                         changed = true;
    6755              :                 }

    6757        25769 :         else if (TransactionIdIsNormal(xid))

    6759            0 :                 if (TransactionIdPrecedes(xid, relfrozenxid))

    6766              :                 {

    6772              :                          */
    6773            0 :                         if (!HEAP_XMAX_IS_LOCKED_ONLY(tuple->t_infomask) &&
    6774            0 :                                 TransactionIdDidCommit(xid))

    6857              : /*
    6858              :  * heap_execute_freeze_tuple
    6859              :  *              Execute the prepared freezing of a tuple.
    6860              :  *
    6861              :  * Caller is responsible for ensuring that no other backend can access the
    6862              :  * storage underlying this tuple, either by holding an exclusive lock on the
    6863              :  * buffer containing it (which is what lazy VACUUM does), or by having it be
    6864              :  * in private storage (which is what CLUSTER and friends do).
    6865              :  *

    6867              :  * TransactionId read/write is assumed atomic anyway.  However there is a race
    6868              :  * condition: someone who just fetched an old XID that we overwrite here could
    6869              :  * conceivably not finish checking the XID against pg_xact before we finish
    6870              :  * the VACUUM and perhaps truncate off the part of pg_xact he needs.  Getting
    6871              :  * exclusive lock ensures no other backend is in process of checking the
    6872              :  * tuple status.  Also, getting exclusive lock makes it safe to adjust the
    6873              :  * infomask bits.

    6886            0 :                 HeapTupleHeaderSetXvac(tuple, InvalidTransactionId);

    6888        12547 :         tuple->t_infomask = frz->t_infomask;

    6892              : /*

    7149              : /*
    7150              :  * Do_MultiXactIdWait
    7151              :  *              Actual implementation for the two functions below.
    7152              :  *
    7153              :  * 'multi', 'status' and 'infomask' indicate what to sleep on (the status is
    7154              :  * needed to ensure we only sleep on conflicting members, and the infomask is
    7155              :  * used to optimize multixact access in case it's a lock-only multi); 'nowait'
    7156              :  * indicates whether to use conditional lock acquisition, to allow callers to
    7157              :  * fail if lock is unavailable.  'rel', 'ctid' and 'oper' are used to set up
    7158              :  * context information for error messages.  'remaining', if not NULL, receives
    7159              :  * the number of members that are still running, including any (non-aborted)
    7160              :  * subtransactions of our own transaction.
    7161              :  *
    7162              :  * We do this by sleeping on each member using XactLockTableWait.  Any
    7163              :  * members that belong to the current backend are *not* waited for, however;
    7164              :  * this would not merely be useless but would lead to Assert failure inside
    7165              :  * XactLockTableWait.  By the time this returns, it is certain that all
    7166              :  * transactions *of other backends* that were members of the MultiXactId
    7167              :  * that conflict with the requested status are dead (and no new ones can have
    7168              :  * been added, since it is not legal to add members to an existing
    7169              :  * MultiXactId).
    7170              :  *
    7171              :  * But by the time we finish sleeping, someone else may have changed the Xmax
    7172              :  * of the containing tuple, so the caller needs to iterate on us somehow.
    7173              :  *
    7174              :  * Note that in case we return false, the number of remaining members is
    7175              :  * not to be trusted.
    7176              :  */

    7178            0 : Do_MultiXactIdWait(MultiXactId multi, MultiXactStatus status,

    7181              :                                    int *remaining)
    7182              : {
    7183            0 :         bool            result = true;

    7191            0 :                                                           HEAP_XMAX_IS_LOCKED_ONLY(infomask));

    7195              :                 int                     i;

    7233            0 :                                 XactLockTableWait(memxid, rel, ctid, oper);
    7234              :                 }
    7235              : 
    7236            0 :                 pfree(members);
    7237              :         }
    7238              : 
    7239            0 :         if (remaining)

    7241              : 
    7242            0 :         return result;

    7245              : /*
    7246              :  * MultiXactIdWait

    7251              :  *
    7252              :  * We return (in *remaining, if not NULL) the number of members that are still

    7254              :  */
    7255              : static void
    7256            0 : MultiXactIdWait(MultiXactId multi, MultiXactStatus status, uint16 infomask,
    7257              :                                 Relation rel, ItemPointer ctid, XLTW_Oper oper,
    7258              :                                 int *remaining)
    7259              : {

    7264              : /*
    7265              :  * ConditionalMultiXactIdWait
    7266              :  *              As above, but only lock if we can get the lock without blocking.
    7267              :  *
    7268              :  * By the time we finish sleeping, someone else may have changed the Xmax

    7270              :  *

    7272              :  * transactions might still be running.
    7273              :  *

    7275              :  * running, including any (non-aborted) subtransactions of our own transaction.
    7276              :  */

    7278            0 : ConditionalMultiXactIdWait(MultiXactId multi, MultiXactStatus status,

    7282              :                                                           rel, NULL, XLTW_None, remaining);

    7501              : /*
    7502              :  * Get the latestRemovedXid from the heap pages pointed at by the index

    7504              :  *

    7506              :  * approach now appears inferior.  It meant that the master could generate
    7507              :  * a lot of work for the standby without any back-pressure to slow down the
    7508              :  * master, and it required the standby to have reached consistency, whereas
    7509              :  * we want to have correct information available even before that point.
    7510              :  *

    7512              :  * deleting hundreds of tuples from a single index block.  To amortize that

    7702              : 
    7703              : /*
    7704              :  * Perform XLogInsert for a heap-clean operation.  Caller must already
    7705              :  * have modified the buffer and marked it dirty.
    7706              :  *

    7708              :  * zero-based tuple indexes.  Now they are one-based like other uses
    7709              :  * of OffsetNumber.
    7710              :  *

    7712              :  * the removed tuples. That allows recovery processing to cancel or wait
    7713              :  * for long standby queries that can still see these tuples.
    7714              :  */

    7758              :

    7868              :         /*

    7872              :          * the beginning and end of the tuple.  That's quick to check, and
    7873              :          * perfectly covers the common case that only one field is updated.

    7878              :          * some reason, it would allow the corruption to propagate the new page,
    7879              :          * so it seems best to avoid.  Under the general assumption that most
    7880              :          * updates tend to create the new tuple version on the same page, there

    7882              :          *

    8130              : /*
    8131              :  * Build a heap tuple representing the configured REPLICA IDENTITY to represent

    8135              :  * key defined.

    8138              :  * any of them have any external data.  Delete must always pass true.

    8154         6395 :         *copy = false;

    8156         6395 :         if (!RelationIsLogicallyLogged(relation))

    8159            0 :         if (replident == REPLICA_IDENTITY_NOTHING)

    8161              : 
    8162            0 :         if (replident == REPLICA_IDENTITY_FULL)
    8163              :         {
    8164              :                 /*

    8167              :                  */
    8168            0 :                 if (HeapTupleHasExternal(tp))
    8169              :                 {
    8170            0 :                         *copy = true;
    8171            0 :                         tp = toast_flatten_tuple(tp, desc);
    8172              :                 }
    8173            0 :                 return tp;
    8174              :         }
    8175              : 
    8176              :         /* if the key isn't required and we're only logging the key, we're done */
    8177            0 :         if (!key_required)
    8178            0 :                 return NULL;
    8179              : 
    8180              :         /* find out the replica identity columns */

    8182              :                                                                                  INDEX_ATTR_BITMAP_IDENTITY_KEY);
    8183              : 
    8184              :         /*
    8185              :          * If there's no defined replica identity columns, treat as !key_required.

    8188              :          * constant true for key_required, so we can hit this case in deletes.)
    8189              :          */
    8190            0 :         if (bms_is_empty(idattrs))
    8191            0 :                 return NULL;
    8192              : 
    8193              :         /*
    8194              :          * Construct a new tuple containing only the replica identity columns,
    8195              :          * with nulls elsewhere.  While we're at it, assert that the replica
    8196              :          * identity columns aren't null.

    8200            0 :         for (int i = 0; i < desc->natts; i++)

    8203              :                                                   idattrs))

    8206            0 :                         nulls[i] = true;
    8207              :         }
    8208              : 
    8209            0 :         key_tuple = heap_form_tuple(desc, values, nulls);

    8214              :         /*

    8232              : /*
    8233              :  * Handles CLEANUP_INFO

    8253              : /*
    8254              :  * Handles XLOG_HEAP2_CLEAN record type
    8255              :  */
    8256              : static void
    8257            0 : heap_xlog_clean(XLogReaderState *record)
    8258              : {
    8259            0 :         XLogRecPtr      lsn = record->EndRecPtr;
    8260            0 :         xl_heap_clean *xlrec = (xl_heap_clean *) XLogRecGetData(record);

    8266            0 :         XLogRecGetBlockTag(record, 0, &rnode, NULL, &blkno);
    8267              : 
    8268              :         /*

    8341              : /*
    8342              :  * Replay XLOG_HEAP2_VISIBLE record.
    8343              :  *
    8344              :  * The critical integrity requirement here is that we must never end up with

    8346              :  * PD_ALL_VISIBLE bit is clear.  If that were to occur, then a subsequent

    8348              :  */

    8350            0 : heap_xlog_visible(XLogReaderState *record)
    8351              : {
    8352            0 :         XLogRecPtr      lsn = record->EndRecPtr;
    8353            0 :         xl_heap_visible *xlrec = (xl_heap_visible *) XLogRecGetData(record);
